title: Vanguard vs. State Street: Which Consumer Staples ETF Stands Out?
author: Erin Kennedy
description: Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC) and State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) offer similar defensive exposure to essential goods companies. VDC provides broader diversification with 103 holdings, while XLP focuses on 35 large-cap S&P 500 companies. Both have comparable expense ratios (~0.09%), similar low volatility profiles, and nearly identical top holdings. XLP offers slightly higher dividend yield (2.6% vs 2.2%) and greater trading liquidity, while VDC provides more diversification benefits.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Offers broader diversification with 103 holdings versus competitor's 35, providing better risk management. Slightly higher 5-year growth ($1,428 vs $1,382 on $1,000 investment) and comparable expense ratio make it attractive for diversification-focused investors.

Offers marginally lower expense ratio (0.08%), higher dividend yield (2.6%), larger AUM ($13.8B), and significantly higher trading volume (25x more than VDC), making it more liquid and appealing for income-focused investors seeking easier entry/exit.
Mentioned as a top holding in both ETFs (11.19% in XLP, 14.49% in VDC), indicating strong representation in consumer staples sector but no specific performance commentary provided.
Listed as second-largest holding in both ETFs (9.19% in XLP, 11.83% in VDC), demonstrating significant portfolio weight but no distinct analysis or commentary provided.
Included as third-largest holding in both ETFs (7.49% in XLP, 8.69% in VDC), representing core consumer staples exposure but without specific performance insights.

title: Winning AI Search: A Marketer’s Guide to Press Releases and Earned Media
author: Not Specified
description: The American Marketing Association and Notified are hosting a webinar on June 30, 2026, to discuss how press releases and earned media help brands achieve visibility in AI-powered search results. The session will cover how to optimize content for AI discoverability and cite a case study of revenue growth through AI-optimized press releases.

title: Consumer Staples ETFs: How PBJ and XLP Stack Up
author: Jake Lerch
description: The article compares two consumer staples ETFs: XLP (State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF) and PBJ (Invesco Food & Beverage ETF). XLP offers a broader consumer staples exposure with a significantly lower expense ratio (0.08% vs 0.61%), higher dividend yield (2.6% vs 1.6%), and superior 5-year performance ($1,344 vs $1,174 on $1,000 invested). PBJ focuses narrowly on food and beverage stocks with a more concentrated portfolio. Both are defensive investments with low volatility, but XLP emerges as the stronger choice for most investors due to its cost efficiency, higher returns, and larger asset base.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: XLP is presented as the superior choice with significantly lower expense ratio (0.08%), higher dividend yield (2.6%), stronger 5-year total returns ($1,344 vs $1,174), larger AUM ($14.7B), and broader diversification across the consumer staples sector.

PBJ is presented as a viable but less attractive alternative with higher expense ratio (0.61%), lower dividend yield (1.6%), weaker performance, and narrower focus on food and beverage stocks. It offers more concentrated exposure but underperforms XLP on most metrics.
Mentioned as a major holding (11.12%) in XLP, representing a significant position in the fund's portfolio without specific performance commentary.
Listed as a top holding (9.21%) in XLP as a consumer staples heavyweight without specific sentiment attached.
Mentioned as a major XLP holding (7.27%) and noted as a consumer giant, included without specific performance commentary.
Listed as a top holding in PBJ (4.98%) without specific sentiment, representing the food and beverage focus of that fund.

title: Worried About a Stock Market Crash? 2 Magnificant Dividend Stocks You Can Buy Today and Hold Forever
author: Jennifer Saibil
description: With the S&P 500 up 9% year-to-date but valuations appearing stretched, investors concerned about a market correction should consider dividend stocks for portfolio stability. Procter & Gamble and Coca-Cola are highlighted as reliable dividend-paying companies with strong track records of raising dividends through market cycles, offering both growth and passive income.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Recognized as a Dividend King with 70 consecutive years of dividend increases, demonstrating exceptional reliability and durability. Strong brand portfolio provides pricing power and resilience. Recent quarter showed 7% sales growth and solid earnings performance.

Also a Dividend King with strong recent performance, up 19% year-to-date while beating the market. Demonstrated pricing power with 12% revenue growth and 19% operating income increase in recent quarter. Significant growth opportunity in underdeveloped markets and AI-driven strategy implementation.

title: First Trust (FTXG) Vs. iShares (IYK): Is a Food & Beverage Focus the Better ETF Option for Investors?
author: Josh Kohn-Lindquist
description: A comparison of two defensive equity ETFs reveals that iShares U.S. Consumer Staples ETF (IYK) outperforms First Trust Nasdaq Food & Beverage ETF (FTXG) with lower expenses (0.38% vs 0.60%), higher 5-year returns ($1,364 vs $955 on $1,000 invested), and broader sector exposure. While FTXG offers a niche food and beverage focus, IYK's superior performance and lower costs make it the more attractive option for conservative investors.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: IYK demonstrates superior performance with 8.8% annualized returns since 2000, lower expense ratio (0.38%), higher 5-year growth ($1,364 vs $955), smaller maximum drawdown (15.10% vs 21.70%), and broader diversification across consumer staples sector with 54 holdings.

FTXG underperforms with only 3.2% annualized returns since 2016, higher expense ratio (0.60%), worse 5-year performance, and faces headwinds from GLP-1 drugs affecting food and beverage consumption. The author explicitly states it is 'less palatable' than IYK.
Mentioned as a top holding (13.51%) in IYK portfolio; no specific sentiment expressed about the company itself.
Held in both ETFs as a significant position; mentioned as a top holding but no specific performance commentary provided.
Listed as a top IYK holding (11.02%); noted as a cigarette/vape stock that some investors may wish to avoid, but no inherent negative sentiment about the company.
